title: Ethics in Artificial Intelligence
description: A branching course exploring bias, transparency, and accountability in AI systems.

nodes.title: Understanding Bias in Artificial Intelligence
nodes.content: Bias in AI occurs when machine learning models produce systematically prejudiced results due to biased training data, flawed algorithms, or problematic design choices. This happens because AI systems learn patterns from historical data, which often reflects existing societal inequalities. For example, if training data contains hiring decisions biased against certain groups, the AI learns to replicate that bias. Understanding bias is crucial because AI decisions increasingly affect loan approvals, job hiring, criminal sentencing, and healthcare—making fair, unbiased AI systems essential for equitable society.

nodes.title: Facial Recognition Bias: A Real-World Case
nodes.content: Major tech companies' facial recognition systems showed significantly higher error rates for darker-skinned individuals compared to lighter-skinned ones. This occurred because training datasets contained disproportionately more images of lighter-skinned faces. Researchers discovered these systems misidentified Black women up to 34% of the time while achieving 99% accuracy on white males. This example illustrates how historical data imbalances directly translate into algorithmic bias, with serious consequences for justice and civil liberties when used in law enforcement.

nodes.title: Bias in AI Assessment
nodes.content: Test your understanding of how bias originates and manifests in artificial intelligence systems.

questions.question: What is the primary source of bias in machine learning models?
options: The algorithms are deliberately programmed to discriminate
Training data that reflects historical inequalities and imbalances
The speed at which computers process information
The programming language used to build the system
feedback: Incorrect. Most AI bias is not intentional.
Correct! AI systems learn from and perpetuate patterns in their training data.
Incorrect. Processing speed doesn't cause bias.
Incorrect. The programming language is not the source of bias.
questions.explanation: Bias in AI primarily originates from the training data used. If historical data contains biased patterns—such as underrepresentation of certain groups or past discriminatory practices—the AI model learns and reproduces these biases.

questions.question: Why is bias in facial recognition systems particularly concerning in law enforcement?
options: It slows down criminal investigations
It increases government surveillance budgets
It can lead to wrongful arrests, disproportionately affecting minorities
It makes mugshots less clear
feedback: Incorrect. Speed is not the primary concern.
Incorrect. This isn't the main ethical issue.
Correct! Biased facial recognition directly threatens justice and civil liberties.
Incorrect. Image quality is not the ethical concern here.
questions.explanation: In law enforcement, biased facial recognition can lead to wrongful arrests and disproportionately harm innocent people from marginalized groups, undermining justice and fundamental rights.

nodes.title: Transparency and Accountability in AI Systems
nodes.content: Transparency means making AI decision-making processes understandable to users and stakeholders, while accountability ensures responsibility for AI systems' outcomes. Many AI models, especially deep neural networks, operate as 'black boxes'—their decisions cannot be easily explained even by their creators. Transparency is essential so affected individuals understand why decisions about them were made. Accountability mechanisms ensure organizations take responsibility for harms caused by their AI and have processes to correct errors. Together, these principles build trust and enable oversight of AI systems affecting rights and welfare.

nodes.title: Medical AI Diagnosis Without Explanation
nodes.content: A hospital implements an AI system for cancer diagnosis that achieves 95% accuracy. However, when doctors ask why the system flagged a particular patient as high-risk, it cannot explain its reasoning—only provide a probability score. This lack of transparency creates problems: doctors cannot verify if the reasoning is sound, patients cannot understand their diagnosis, and if the system is wrong, no one can identify why or how to correct it. Adding explainability features revealed the system relied partly on irrelevant patient information, highlighting why transparency is critical for high-stakes medical decisions.

nodes.title: Transparency and Accountability Assessment
nodes.content: Evaluate your understanding of transparency, explainability, and accountability in AI systems.

questions.question: What does 'black box' mean in the context of AI?
options: An AI system stored in a physical black container
A system whose decision-making process cannot be easily understood or explained
An AI that only uses dark-themed user interfaces
A system designed to hide information from users
feedback: Incorrect. It's not about physical containers.
Correct! Black box refers to systems where we cannot see how decisions are made.
Incorrect. Color theme is unrelated to transparency.
Incorrect. Black box is about technical interpretability, not intentional hiding.
questions.explanation: A black box AI system is one where we cannot understand or explain how it arrived at its decisions, even though we can observe the inputs and outputs. This is common in deep learning systems with millions of parameters.

questions.question: Why is accountability important in AI systems used for loan decisions?
options: It makes loan processing faster
It allows people to understand and challenge decisions that affect their finances and credit
It guarantees that everyone will receive a loan
It reduces the cost of loan applications
feedback: Incorrect. Accountability is not about speed.
Correct! Accountability in loan AI ensures fairness and allows people to challenge unfair decisions.
Incorrect. Accountability doesn't guarantee approvals.
Incorrect. Accountability doesn't directly affect application costs.
questions.explanation: In high-stakes decisions like loan approvals, accountability ensures organizations take responsibility for their AI systems and provide mechanisms for affected individuals to understand, challenge, and appeal decisions.

nodes.title: Ethics in AI: Practice and Review
nodes.content: Reinforce your understanding of core ethical principles in artificial intelligence through these practice questions.

questions.question: Which of the following is the best approach to address algorithmic bias?
options: Ignore historical data patterns in AI training
Use diverse, representative training data and continuously audit for bias
Remove all humans from AI decision-making processes
Only use AI in decisions where bias doesn't matter
feedback: Incorrect. Ignoring data doesn't solve bias.
Correct! Diverse data and continuous auditing are essential bias-mitigation strategies.
Incorrect. Human oversight is important for ethical AI.
Incorrect. Bias mitigation matters everywhere AI affects people.
questions.explanation: Addressing bias requires using representative training data that reflects the diversity of the population affected, combined with ongoing monitoring and auditing to catch and fix bias as it emerges.

questions.question: A bank's AI loan system denies a loan application automatically. Which principle is violated if the applicant cannot learn why?
options: Efficiency
Transparency and accountability
Data security
Processing speed
feedback: Incorrect. Efficiency isn't the primary ethical concern.
Correct! Without explanation, the system lacks transparency and accountability.
Incorrect. Security isn't directly violated here.
Incorrect. Speed is not the ethical issue.
questions.explanation: Transparency requires that affected individuals understand the reasoning behind decisions. Accountability means organizations can explain and take responsibility for those decisions, enabling people to challenge unfair outcomes.

nodes.title: What Would You Like to Do Next?
nodes.content: You've successfully completed the core modules on ethics in AI. Choose your next step:

nodes.title: Course Complete: Ethics in AI
nodes.content: Congratulations! You've completed the Ethics in Artificial Intelligence course. You now understand how bias originates in AI systems and why transparency and accountability are essential for ethical AI. These principles—addressing bias, ensuring transparency, and establishing accountability—are foundational to building fair, trustworthy AI systems. As AI becomes more prevalent in society, your understanding of these ethics will be increasingly important for responsible AI development and deployment.
